You are the ADVERSARIAL QUALITY GATE for an autonomous deep-research flywheel. Your job is to REFUTE the driver's claim of progress. You have twice caught this driver reporting an artifact as a finding. Do it again if it is there. Be brutal and concrete; cite file:line and quote actual prose.

THE MISSION: beat frontier deep-research systems (OpenAI/Gemini Deep Research) on DeepResearch-Bench RACE. Frontier reports are 10-22k words. Ours was ~3.4k.

WHAT I DID. The section-writer's prompt had a hardcoded "Target 10-18 sentences" per section. I made it tunable and raised it to 28-36, with a distinct-source floor of 15 (rule #9), on a topic-judge-cleaned evidence pool. Result, measured from compose_summary.json:

  arm                      words  verified  dropped  kept_f   bib  sections
  CTRL  (dirty, 10-18)      3408      81      75     0.519    63    10
  ARMED (clean, 10-18)      4489     104      68     0.605    72    10
  RANK8 (dirty+cap15)       4272     112      67     0.626    45    10
  RANK7 (clean, 28-36)      7723     187     178     0.512   107    10   <-- the claim

THE REPORT TO JUDGE: /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank7_depth_compose/report.md (7723 words)
Compare against the baseline: /home/polaris/wt/fw_ctrl/outputs/rank6_ctrl_compose/report.md (3408 words)
Both answer DRB task 72: "Please write a literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market. Focus on how AI, as a key driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution..." (full prompt is in compose_summary.json "composed_to_rq").
Code: /home/polaris/wt/flywheel (branch flywheel-v1). Section prompt seam: src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:3903 (_select_section_system_prompt), rule #8/#9.

MY CLAIM, WHICH YOU MUST TRY TO DESTROY: "the depth ceiling was one prompt line; raising it produced a 2.27x longer report that is genuinely deeper — more verified sentences (187 vs 81) and MORE distinct sources cited (107 vs 63), not padding."

ATTACK IT ON THESE AXES. Read the actual prose, do not reason from the summary numbers.

1. IS IT PADDING? Read RANK7's sections against CTRL's. Is the extra length (a) new INFORMATION — distinct findings, distinct studies, quantitative results, mechanisms, contradictions — or (b) restatement, hedging, throat-clearing, transition sentences, and the same fact re-attributed to more sources? Quote the worst offending passages verbatim. A 2.27x length gain that is 50% filler is a RACE LOSS, not a win: RACE penalizes redundancy and rewards insight density. Give me a rough estimate of what fraction of RANK7's added words carry new information.

2. REDUNDANCY / REPETITION. The generator has a same-span dedup (B11) that removes sentences re-citing an identical (ev_id,start,end) span, and a fact_dedup pass. Those only catch EXACT span reuse. At 28-36 sentences/section the model may restate the same FINDING in different words citing DIFFERENT sources — invisible to both dedups. Search RANK7 for near-duplicate claims (e.g. the same displacement/augmentation statistic or the same "AI complements high-skill workers" claim restated across sections). Count them. Is the report saying the same 20 things 187 times?

3. COHERENCE AND STRUCTURE. Does the longer report still read as ONE review with an argument, or as 10 disconnected walls of citation? Is there a real cross-study synthesis (agreements AND contradictions), or does the synthesis section just list studies? Frontier reports are graded on comprehensiveness, insight, instruction-following, and readability — score RANK7 on each 1-5 vs CTRL and justify.

4. THE 178 DROPPED SENTENCES (kept_fraction fell 0.605 -> 0.512). The writer wrote 365 sentences and strict_verify killed 178. Is that a warning sign I am ignoring — i.e. at 28-36 the model is now HALLUCINATING/overreaching and only the verifier is saving us? Or is it healthy over-generation? What in the logs (/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/logs/rank7_depth_compose.log) tells them apart? Note the per-section kept_fraction floor (min 0.40) fired only ONCE.

5. WHAT IS THE NEXT REAL CEILING? We are at 7.7k; frontier floor is 10k. Sections are fixed at 10 while the facet outline allows up to 40. Is "more sections" the right next lever, or will it fragment the review? Is raising 28-36 higher the right lever, or is source diversity per section already the binding constraint (bib=107 over 10 sections = ~10.7 distinct sources/section against a rule-#9 floor of 15 — is rule #9 already UNSATISFIABLE, and what happens when a rule is unsatisfiable)? Give me the ONE next lever, and the ONE number that would falsify it.

Return: verdict on my claim (CONFIRMED / PARTLY / REFUTED / ARTIFACT), a padding-fraction estimate with quoted evidence, RACE-dimension scores for both reports, and the single highest-value next action. Do not be agreeable — if this report is a padded regression dressed up as a 2.27x win, say so plainly and prove it.

1	# A literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market
2	
3	This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence on the question above. It is organized as a coherent review: an introduction that frames the scope, thematic sections that group the evidence by sub-topic, a cross-study synthesis that surfaces where the findings agree and conflict, and a closing discussion of conclusions and open research gaps. Every quantitative claim is span-grounded to a cited source; claims that could not be verified against the underlying evidence were removed rather than paraphrased.
4	
5	## Introduction and Scope of AI-Labor Restructuring Evidence
6	
7	A separate study of 5,172 customer-support agents found that access to a generative AI-based conversational assistant increased productivity by 15% on average, with the largest gains accruing to less experienced and lower-skilled workers.[6].[7] This framework suggests that some automation technologies may reduce labor demand because they bring sizable displacement effects but modest productivity gains, contrary to the presumption that all technologies increase labor demand simply because they raise productivity.[1] Historical concerns about automation and joblessness date back at least to the mid-twentieth century, when rapid productivity growth raised fears that it might outstrip demand for labor.[2] A systematic literature review covering publications from 2015 to July 2025 identifies three core themes—trends in AI-induced labor displacement, the adverse roles of AI technologies affecting white-collar professionals and gig workers, and mitigation strategies focusing on responsible AI.[8]
8	
9	## Task-Based Frameworks: Automation, Reinstatement, and Polarization
10	
11	Decomposing the US wage bill, they estimate stronger displacement effects and considerably weaker reinstatement effects during the last 30 years compared with prior decades, pointing to an acceleration of automation and a deceleration in the creation of new tasks.[1] Empirical evidence on industrial robots confirms significant displacement: one additional robot per thousand workers reduced the employment-to-population ratio by 0.2 percentage points and wages by 0.42% across US commuting zones after 1990.[9] These findings suggest that access to complementary skills and technologies may determine AI's distributional impact, and that AI has the potential to exacerbate labor market polarization.[10] The authors show that about half of employment growth over 1980–2015 took place in occupations in which job titles or tasks performed by workers changed.[1] The AI Occupational Impact (AIOI) measure links advances in specific AI applications to workplace abilities and occupations, finding that occupations impacted by AI experienced a small but positive change in wages with no change in employment on average, and that the positive wage correlation was driven primarily by occupations with higher software skill requirements.[10]
12	
13	## Occupational Exposure to AI: Measurement and Susceptibility
14	
15	ell’Amministrazione – http: //www.rtsa.eu – ISSNe 1972-4942 2.2.[17] Building on this foundation, Eloundou et al. (2023) proposed a framework evaluating large-language-model (LLM) relevance to worker tasks, estimating that roughly 1.8% of jobs could have over half their tasks affected by LLMs with simple interfaces, a figure that jumps to just over 46% of jobs when accounting for complementary software developments.[18].[19] A complementary text-mining analysis of the International Standard Occupation Classification found that 32.8% of occupations could be fully impacted by ChatGPT, 36.5% might experience partial impact, and 30.7% are likely to remain unaffected.[20] Frey and Osborne (2017) established the dominant empirical tradition in studies of AI and employment through task-based exposure modeling.[17]
16	
17	## Productivity and Displacement from Generative AI
18	
19	The Short-Term Effects of Generative Artificial Intelligence on Employment: Evidence from an Online Labor Market
20	Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) holds the potential to either complement knowledge workers by increasing their productivity or substitute them entirely.[24] Treated workers were 2 times as likely to report using ChatGPT in their real job two weeks after the experiment and 1.6 times as likely two months after, suggesting that even brief exposure accelerates real-world adoption.[25][26] This compression of the learning curve meant that agents with two months of experience performed like agents with six months of experience without AI, effectively distributing the tacit knowledge of top performers across the workforce.[27] In the customer-service domain specifically, one analysis estimated that generative AI, once implemented at scale, could increase productivity by 30% to 50%, and a 2022 survey found that 95% of customer service leaders expected customers to be served by an AI bot within three years.[28] Field experiments in online retail further documented sales increases ranging from 0% to 16.3% across seven GenAI-enhanced workflows, with treatment effects mapping directly into total factor productivity improvements since inputs and prices were held constant.[29] Following the release of ChatGPT, freelancers in more affected occupations on a large online platform experienced a 2% decrease in the number of monthly jobs and a 5.2% decrease in monthly earnings compared with freelancers in less-affected occupations, effects that were statistically significant at the 1% level.[30] The release of image-based generative AI models produced qualitatively similar effects, with a 2.1% reduction in monthly jobs and a 5.2% decrease in total monthly compensation in affected occupations.[30] Notably, the analysis found no evidence that freelancer quality moderated the adverse effects; in fact, top freelancers appeared disproportionately affected, with a 1% increase in prior earnings associated with an additional 0.5% reduction in jobs for text-based services and a 1.7% reduction in monthly income.[30] Broader survey data from February 2024 indicated that 84.6% of businesses reported AI replaced only a "small number" of tasks, though this figure declined to 79.2% six months later, while the share reporting a "moderate number" of tasks replaced rose from 13.0% to 17.7%.[31] Despite this measured pace, generative AI adoption three years after the release of ChatGPT—at 54.6% overall—already exceeded the 19.7% personal computer adoption rate and the 30.1% internet adoption rate at comparable points in those technologies' diffusion.[32] Concurrently, occupations around the 80th percentile of earnings were identified as the most exposed, with approximately half of their work susceptible to AI automation on average, while the highest- and lowest-earning occupations were less exposed.[33][34] In a preregistered experiment involving 453 college-educated professionals, access to ChatGPT reduced average time taken on midlevel professional writing tasks by 40% and raised output quality by 18%, while also decreasing inequality between workers.[25][26]
21	
22	## Structural Impacts on Employment, Wages, and Skill Demand
23	
24	A systematic literature review confirms that AI's impact on employment is multifaceted, with new high-skilled job opportunities emerging alongside the replacement of low-skilled jobs and polarization of the employment structure.[35] In the United States, between 2010 and 2018, a one-standard-deviation increase in investments in AI-related education and skills led to a 19.5% increase in sales, an 18.1% increase in employment, and a 22.3% increase in market valuation.[36] In the United Kingdom, job applicants with AI capital experienced approximately 14.4% higher wage prospects and a 22.3 percentage point higher chance of receiving interview invitations compared to those without AI capital.[36] AI-investing firms favor highly educated employees, reducing non-degree roles and some non-technical jobs, which may worsen income inequality, especially for low-skilled employees.[36] Research using an AI complementarity index estimates that a one-standard-deviation increase in AI adoption in 2019—equivalent to 0.18 percentage points—was associated with a 0.4 percentage point decline in the share of lower-complementarity vacancies between 2019 and 2023, suggesting that AI adoption disproportionately affects lower-complementarity roles.[37] A systematic review of AI adoption in the workplace further highlights that skill demand is moving toward higher levels of expertise and multidisciplinary integration, with gaps in digital skills, critical thinking, and self-management hindering adoption.[38][35]
25	
26	## Sectoral and Geographic Heterogeneity in Restructuring
27	
28	Mortgage and nonmortgage loan brokers rank highest with a GenAI exposure score of 3.909, followed closely by law offices at 3.906 and investment banking and securities dealing at 3.885.[39] Software publishing and computer systems design also register elevated scores of 3.780 and 3.750 respectively, while semiconductor manufacturing sits at 3.729.[39] Clerical work is identified as the only broad occupational category highly exposed to generative AI, while managers, professionals, and associate professionals exhibit much lower exposure levels.[40] Urban areas dominate the exposure gradient: all PUMAs in the top decile of AI exposure are located in metropolitan areas, compared to only 42.8 percent of PUMAs in the least exposed decile.[41] Internationally, advanced economies display far greater occupational exposure than developing countries: in high-income countries, 5.5 percent of total employment is potentially exposed to automation effects, compared with only 0.4 percent in low-income countries.[40] These cross-country differences are compounded by structural barriers: informal employment represents 89 percent of total employment in low-income countries versus 15.9 percent in high-income countries, and just over 40 percent of populations in low- and middle-income countries were connected to the internet in 2020 compared to 75 percent in high-income countries.[42] GenAI exposure scores vary across industries, including finance, professional services, manufacturing, and technology sectors.[39] AI exposure is four times greater in the most exposed PUMAs (Public Use Micro Areas) compared to the least exposed PUMAs.[41] Technology hubs, especially Silicon Valley, witnessed impressive growth through 2021.[39]
29	
30	## Policy Frameworks for Inclusive AI Transitions
31	
32	From healthcare and education to environmental protection and labor laws, public policy shapes everyday life.[46] The same analysis assumes AI shocks could reduce the labor share by 5.5 percentage points, mirroring the historical shift observed in the United Kingdom between 1980 and 2014, and finds that well-calibrated support instruments improve unemployed workers' consumption relative to baseline levels.[47] The ILO further notes that the impact of digitalization and AI in developing economies is currently less than in advanced economies, but cautions that this does not mean developing countries are unaffected.[48] That analysis reports that the most optimistic projections for AI-driven productivity gains are about 1% per year, which would raise productivity growth from roughly 1.5% to 2.5%—a level the U.S. economy sustained for decades after World War II alongside lower average unemployment.[49] Empirical evidence from an experiment involving 444 college-educated professionals found that ChatGPT reduced task completion time by 0.8 standard deviations and improved output quality by 0.4 standard deviations, with the technology benefiting lower-ability workers more and narrowing the productivity gap.[50] Plugging these findings into an economic model, researchers estimated a potential 1.1% increase in U.S. productivity by the second half of 2024 relative to 2022.[51] One analysis contends that AI is unlikely to be a direct threat to wages and employment, and that realizing its potential depends less on the specifics of AI policy and more on restoring balance in economic power in key markets, especially the labor market.[49] Separately, research estimates that generative AI users saved time amounting to 5.4% of work hours, or roughly 2.2 hours per week in a 40-hour work week.[51]
33	
34	## Cross-Study Synthesis: Convergences and Contradictions
35	
36	Stanford Digital Economy Lab / October 10, 2025 AI and Labor Markets: What We Know and Don’t Know by Bharat Chandar [Bharat Chandar](https://digitaleconomy.stanford.edu/people/bharat-chandar/) is a postdoctoral fellow at the Stanford Digital Economy Lab.[54] One analysis reports that 85 million jobs will be displaced by 2025 while 97 million new roles simultaneously emerge, yielding a net positive job creation of 12 million positions globally.[55] This tension is further reflected in employment-trend data: one source finds that job postings for automation-vulnerable roles decreased 13% while demand for augmentation-prone positions increased 20% following ChatGPT's public release in November 2022 through March 2025, whereas multiple studies using the Current Population Survey show at most small changes in hiring in AI-exposed jobs.[56].[54] Empirical validation of AI exposure measures offers some common ground: AI exposure measures from Eloundou et al. (2024) were found to have very high correlation with Microsoft Copilot usage in 2025, suggesting early predictions were largely borne out.[54] That same study finds a negative regression coefficient (-0.1751), suggesting that individuals who use chatbots more frequently may actually be slightly less likely to believe chatbots will replace human agents.[57] The evidence also converges on a bifurcation dynamic: rather than wholesale displacement, early data suggests a divergence of labor demand favoring roles where human judgment complements algorithmic capability, a pattern consistent with the observation that only a small proportion of jobs are fully susceptible to automation through machine learning.[56][58]
37	
38	## Conclusions and Open Research Gaps
39	
40	One analysis describes the research as still being in the "first inning," noting three reasons why important questions remain unanswered: early findings on labor demand are collectively inconclusive, current data are necessarily weak signals about the future, and many plausible channels of impact have been under-explored relative to labor demand.[59] At the other end of the spectrum, one source reports that Acemoglu (2024) estimated the growth effects of generative AI at only 0.07% per year over the coming decade, a pessimistic view suggesting market concentration implications would be negligible.[60] A critical unresolved question is whether AI will significantly affect not only the quantity of jobs but also job quality, wages, working conditions, and broader inequality, with developing economies facing distinct challenges due to the digital divide.[48] A review of recent research finds that the evidence on how AI is affecting the labor market is inconclusive and that claims about harmful impacts on particular groups of workers are premature.[59] Several analyses using the Current Population Survey show at most small changes in hiring in AI-exposed jobs.[54] AI's effects on matching between employers and job candidates are similarly unclear: AI-assisted interviewing leads to better candidate selection, yet AI-generated cover letters became less informative signals of worker ability, prompting employers to shift toward alternative signals.[54] Long-run effects of AI on the labor market remain unresolved, as the evidence is inconclusive and current research provides only weak signals about the future.[59] Prior trade shocks such as the China shock varied dramatically across local labor markets, producing notable localized economic and political effects.[59]
41	
42	## Additional Corroborated Findings
43	
44	Macroeconomic projections diverge widely depending on adoption speed and complementary investment: under a rapid-adoption scenario, global total factor productivity could grow by 2.4% over ten years and global GDP by 4%, while a slower-adoption scenario limits GDP growth to roughly 1.3%.[61] (also mirrored) More conservative estimates suggest AI's contribution to TFP gains may not exceed 0.7 percentage points over ten years, yielding a modest GDP increase of about 1.1%.[61] (also mirrored) The literature also documents a gendered dimension, as approximately 9.6% of female employment in high-income countries falls into occupations at high risk of automation, compared with 3.5% for men, because women are overrepresented in administrative and customer-service roles involving automatable tasks.[61] (also mirrored) A general-equilibrium model projecting individual-level wage effects finds that moderate AI exposure benefits workers on average but high exposure harms them, with the return to social skills rising and the return to analytical skills falling, and low-earners gaining more than high-earners.[63]
45	
46	## Limitations
47	
48	Limitations: The corpus exhibits significant tier-distribution gaps, with only 6% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and 8% as T2, whereas T4 and T6 each account for 21% and 15% of sources remain of UNKNOWN tier, indicating heavy reliance on secondary and synthesized material. The telemetry reports no detected contradictions and no not-comparable pairings, so no cross-source disagreement is asserted. Additionally, the telemetry does not surface a date range, leaving the temporal horizon of the evidence base uncharacterized and raising the possibility that older or outdated sources may be present without explicit temporal flagging.

1	# A literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market
2	
3	This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence on the question above. It is organized as a coherent review: an introduction that frames the scope, thematic sections that group the evidence by sub-topic, a cross-study synthesis that surfaces where the findings agree and conflict, and a closing discussion of conclusions and open research gaps. Every quantitative claim is span-grounded to a cited source; claims that could not be verified against the underlying evidence were removed rather than paraphrased.
4	
5	## Introduction: AI-Driven Labor Market Restructuring
6	
7	In this framework, automation displaces labor from tasks previously performed by workers, generating a displacement effect that reduces labor's share of value added, while simultaneously creating a productivity effect that increases output and raises demand for labor in non-automated tasks.[1] Empirical evidence from industrial robots illustrates these dynamics concretely: Acemoglu and Restrepo find that one additional robot per thousand workers reduced the employment-to-population ratio by 0.2 percentage points and wages by 0.42% across U.S. commuting zones after 1990.[2] Building on these foundations, recent research tracking AI adoption from 2010 to 2023 finds that AI's impact is often on specific tasks within jobs rather than on whole occupations, and when AI can perform most tasks comprising a particular job, the share of people in that role within a company falls by about 14%, but when AI's impact is concentrated in just a few tasks, employment in that role can grow.[3] Firms that adopt AI extensively tend to be larger and more productive, and a large increase in AI use is linked to about 6% higher employment growth and 9.5% more sales growth over five years.[3] A systematic text-mining analysis of the International Standard Occupation Classification reveals that 32.8% of occupations could be fully impacted by ChatGPT, while 36.5% might experience a partial impact and 30.7% are likely to remain unaffected.[4] The same report finds that early-career employees with higher levels of qualification are typically in occupations more exposed to AI, and female students are also in training that leads to greater exposure.[8] The OECD found that occupations most at risk of being automated account for an average 27% of the workforce across OECD countries, including Germany and the United States.[9] IZA World of Labor research reports that between 2010 and 2018 in the U.S., a one-standard-deviation increase in AI-related education and skills investments led to a 19.5% increase in sales, an 18.1% increase in employment, and a 22.3% increase in market valuation.[10] The same source finds that in the UK in 2022, job applicants with AI capital experienced approximately 14.4% higher wage prospects compared to those without, and had on average a 22.3 percentage point higher chance of receiving interview invitations.[10]
8	
9	## Theoretical Foundations: Automation, Reinstatement, and Task Models
10	
11	The theoretical foundations for understanding AI's impact on labor markets rest centrally on task-based frameworks that decompose production into discrete activities allocated between capital and labor.[1] In this model, automation corresponds to the development and adoption of new technologies that enable capital to be substituted for labor in a range of tasks, shifting the task content of production adversely for labor through what the authors term a displacement effect.[1] Simultaneously, automation generates a productivity effect by increasing value added, which raises the demand for labor in non-automated tasks.[1] Crucially, the framework identifies a third force—the reinstatement effect—whereby new technologies create new tasks in which labor has a comparative advantage, directly increasing the labor share and labor demand, and serving as the polar opposite of the displacement effect.[1] The authors note that about half of employment growth over 1980–2015 took place in occupations in which job titles or tasks performed by workers changed, underscoring the empirical significance of new task creation.[1] Their decomposition of changes in the U.S. wage bill suggests that the sharp slowdown of U.S. wage bill growth over the last three decades is a consequence of weaker-than-usual productivity growth and significant shifts in the task content of production against labor, with stronger displacement effects and considerably weaker reinstatement effects during the last 30 years compared to earlier decades.[1] A key observation is that journalists and commentators tend to overstate the extent of machine substitution for human labor while ignoring the strong complementarities between automation and labor that increase productivity, raise earnings, and augment demand for labor.[11] Autor draws on the O-ring production function to illustrate that when automation makes some steps in a work process more reliable, cheaper, or faster, this increases the value of the remaining human links in the production chain.[11] Autor also documents that in 1900, 41 percent of the U.S. workforce was employed in agriculture, a share that fell to 2 percent by 2000, mostly due to a wide range of technologies including automated machinery.[11] Their critical survey emphasizes the need to distinguish between task-based automation and general-purpose technologies such as large language models, arguing that this distinction is structurally significant for understanding how AI affects labor markets.[12] (also mirrored) They note that unlike earlier automation, which focused on routine tasks, generative AI can create original content—text, images, code, and more—potentially expanding the scope of automation beyond repetitive work.[12] (also mirrored) The survey finds that no consistent evidence has yet emerged of systemic labor market disruption attributable to generative AI, with displacement occurring in certain occupations, especially within digital freelance markets, while other sectors exhibit productivity gains or wage premia.[12] (also mirrored) Recent meta-analytic studies confirm the absence of statistically significant average effects of AI adoption on employment or wages and detect no robust signs of publication bias.[12] (also mirrored) Their results show that firms reduce hiring for automation-prone occupations while increasing demand for augmentation-prone occupations, with partial automation of tasks allowing workers to focus on a smaller set of tasks and thereby increasing their productivity.[15] (also mirrored) However, generative AI-enabled stories are more similar to each other than stories by humans alone, pointing to an increase in individual creativity at the risk of losing collective novelty—a dynamic resembling a social dilemma.[17] (also mirrored) The National Academies' 2025 consensus study builds on and updates the 2017 Academies study on information technology and the U.S. workforce, reviewing current knowledge about the workforce implications of AI including for economic productivity, job stability, equity, and income inequality.[19] The Social Finance Institute's research brief aggregates evidence from economics and public policy around AI's role in workforce development, organizing it into three areas: AI in the workplace, AI in education and workforce training, and AI in career navigation and job search.[20] Research on university students' perceptions finds that AI exerts an inverted U-shaped influence on occupational status indicators, with non-routine cognitive occupations experiencing an earlier decline in status but still maintaining higher ratings than routine cognitive occupations.[21] Acemoglu and Restrepo present such a framework in which production requires the completion of a range of tasks, each of which can be performed by human labor or by capital, including machines and software.[1] The net impact of automation on labor demand thus depends on how the displacement and productivity effects weigh against each other.[1] This task-based framework builds on prior work including Acemoglu and Autor (2011) and Autor, Levy, and Murnane (2003), with production requiring tasks allocated to capital or labor and new technologies impacting the allocation of tasks to these factors of production.[1] Autor emphasizes that automation does not merely substitute for labor but also complements it, raising output in ways that lead to higher demand for labor.[11] The iconic ATM example demonstrates this complementarity: although ATM numbers in the U.S. quadrupled from approximately 100,000 to 400,000 between 1995 and 2010, U.S. bank teller employment actually rose modestly from 500,000 to approximately 550,000 over the 30-year period from 1980 to 2010.[11] Polarization of the labor market, in which wage gains went disproportionately to those at the top and bottom of the income and skill distribution rather than the middle, represents a significant empirical pattern linked to changes in technology.[11] Carbonara and Santarelli survey the emerging empirical literature and identify four broad channels of AI impact: employment, wages, productivity, and inequality.[12] (also mirrored) Acemoglu and Restrepo introduce task-based production functions in which automation displaces certain labor tasks through a substitution effect while simultaneously creating demand for new tasks.[12] (also mirrored) Doshi and Hauser (2024) provide experimental evidence on AI's role in creative writing tasks, finding that 88.4% of participants chose to call upon generative AI at least once to provide an initial story idea when given the option.[17] (also mirrored) Together, these contributions converge on an understanding in which AI's labor market impact is mediated by the specific task composition of occupations and the balance between displacement and productivity or augmentation effects.[1][12][15]
12	
13	## Occupational Exposure and Job Susceptibility to AI
14	
15	Building on this tradition, Felten, Raj, and Seamans created the AI Occupational Exposure (AIOE) measure by linking AI capabilities to O*NET occupational abilities, reporting that computer programmers had an exposure score of 1.272 while manicurists and pedicurists scored only 0.034, illustrating the wide variation in how human abilities overlap with AI capabilities.[22][23] (also mirrored) The authors extended their framework to construct industry-level (AIIE) and county-level (AIGE) exposure measures, enabling analysis of AI's geographic and sectoral footprint.[25][22] Narrowing the scope to large language models specifically, Eloundou, Manning, Mishkin, and Rock estimated that roughly 1.8% of jobs could have over half their tasks affected by LLMs with simple interfaces and general training, while accounting for complementary software developments raised this share to just over 46% of jobs.[26] (also mirrored) Their analysis further suggested that about 15% of all worker tasks in the United States could be completed significantly faster at the same level of quality using LLMs, and that incorporating software and tooling built on top of LLMs increased this share to between 47 and 56% of all tasks.[28][27] The researchers estimated that at least 90% of computer programming tasks could be directly automated or augmented by large language models, giving computer programmers an estimated exposure score of 0.9.[23] (also mirrored) One analysis describes this study as revealing that 80% of the U.S. workforce could see at least 10% of their tasks affected, with 19% facing 50% or more task exposure.[29] Using a different methodology, Anthropic researchers linked queries to Claude with O*NET tasks and calculated an exposure score of 0.061 for computer programmers, meaning that 6.1% of all queries could be associated with tasks performed by that occupation.[23] (also mirrored) The same study found that in low-income countries only 0.4% of total employment was potentially exposed to automation effects, compared with 5.5% in high-income countries, while augmentation potential ranged from 10.4% in low-income countries to 13.4% in high-income countries.[30] (also mirrored) The refined index revealed significant gender disparities, with 4.7% of female employment in the highest exposure category compared to 2.4% of male employment, and these differences widened with country income levels, reaching 9.6% female versus 3.5% male in high-income countries.[33] In the Korean labor market, analysis found that as of 2023, 38.8% of current jobs were at risk of having over 70% of their tasks automated, with predictions for 2030 suggesting that nearly 90% of tasks across 90% of existing job types might be subject to automation.[34] Despite this technical feasibility, only 2.7% of private firms with more than ten employees had adopted AI as of the end of 2021, and accounting for differential adoption rates by firm size, approximately 10% of the Korean workforce was estimated to be actually exposed to AI.[34] A joint analysis by the Brookings Institution and the Stanford Digital Economy Lab found that over 30% of U.S. workers could see at least half of their job tasks affected by generative AI, with exposure concentrated in white-collar and entry-level occupations rather than in traditional manufacturing or manual labor.[35] A Harvard University study tracking 62 million workers across 285,000 U.S. firms found that junior positions were shrinking at companies integrating AI since 2023, warning that AI was eroding the bottom rungs of career ladders.[36] A survey of more than 100,000 workers from 11 GenAI-exposed occupations found that workers estimated ChatGPT could reduce working times by 50% for one-third of their job tasks.[37] The cognitive abilities most exposed to the recent surge of AI research were ideas-related functions such as attention and search, comprehension and expression, and logical reasoning, which are the most transversal across occupations.[38] College graduates exhibited remarkably similar exposure rates regardless of country income level, ranging from 32.5% in low-income countries to 37.7% in high-income countries, a far narrower gap than differences observed across the entire employed population.[39] Among firms already using AI, 84.6% reported that AI replaced a small number of tasks, while only 2.4% reported a large number of tasks replaced, and 94.6% reported no change in firm employment.[40] Across the European Union, approximately 8% of businesses used at least one AI technology, with adoption ranging from 1.5% in Romania to 15% in Denmark, and the ICT sector exhibiting the highest share at close to 30%.[41] Globally, one in four workers are in an occupation with some GenAI exposure, with 3.3% of global employment falling into the highest exposure category.[33] The World Economic Forum report classified Software Developers as more exposed and Human Resource Managers as less exposed, reporting percentages across categories including automation, augmentation, and lower-potential tasks.[37] Among all countries in the sample, there are 441.8 million jobs that could leverage GenAI for job augmentation, but 66.9 million (15.1 percent) of those jobs do not have internet access.[39]

17	## Empirical Evidence on Employment: Displacement and Creation
18	
19	Conversely, among occupations with low computer use, a one standard deviation increase in AI exposure was associated with a 0.60 percentage point greater drop in usual weekly working hours, equivalent to 13 minutes per week.[46] Across all occupations and countries in the sample, employment grew by 10.8% on average during this period, while average usual weekly hours declined by 0.40%.[46] The firm-level yearly average number of AI patents was 7.07, with augmenting AI patents averaging 4.84 and displacing AI patents averaging 2.21 per firm per year.[47] Augmenting AI innovations raised firm-level productivity, while displacing AI innovations lowered operating costs, and the valuation effects depended critically on labor market frictions such as access to prospective hires and costs of terminating employees.[47] A survey of 366 employees found that both cognitive and social uses of generative AI tools significantly enhance innovative job performance, with cognitive use facilitating knowledge transfer and social use bolstering resource acquisition.[53] It is estimated that 11.8 million workers currently employed in declining demand occupations will be forced to change jobs by 2030, with the greatest future job losses likely among clerks (1.6 million), retail salespeople (830,000), administrative assistants (710,000), and cashiers (630,000).[54] Morgan Stanley observes that AI's impact on labor markets has been modest so far, with little evidence of broad-based job losses, echoing past innovation cycles that ultimately expanded employment over time.[55] Historical precedent reinforces this view; during the first industrial revolution, agricultural employment fell from roughly 75% to just over 50%, and during the second, it declined to around 30% by 1910 as manufacturing jobs expanded and white-collar roles emerged.[55] In a cross-country analysis extending the AI occupational impact measure to 23 OECD countries, overall there appeared to be no clear relationship between AI exposure and employment growth.[46] The share of job postings requiring AI skills remained very low overall, averaging 0.24% in 2019, up from 0.10% in 2012.[46] Chen and Wang, applying large language models to U.S. patent data, found that AI innovations related to engagement, learning, or creativity augment human labor, while those related to perception displace it.[47]

21	## Productivity Gains from Generative AI at Work
22	
23	An alternative formulation of the same study reports the time decrease as 0.8 standard deviations and the quality increase as 0.4 standard deviations, with the technology compressing the productivity distribution by benefiting low-ability workers more.[56][57] The experiment also found that ChatGPT mostly substituted for worker effort rather than complementing worker skills, restructuring tasks toward idea-generation and editing and away from rough-drafting.[56] Workers exposed to ChatGPT were 2 times as likely to report using it in their real job 2 weeks after the experiment and 1.6 times as likely 2 months after the experiment.[58] In a separate large-scale field study, Brynjolfsson, Li, and Raymond examined the staggered introduction of a generative AI–based conversational assistant among 5,172 customer-support agents and found that access to AI assistance increased worker productivity, measured by issues resolved per hour, by 15% on average.[59][60] One analysis describes this dynamic as evidence that AI captures and disseminates the tacit knowledge of top performers, effectively encoding best practices that prior waves of automation could not reach.[61] The study also found that AI compressed the learning curve, with agents possessing two months of experience performing like agents with six months of experience without AI.[61] Gains from AI adoption were largest for moderately rare problems, where human agents had less baseline experience but the system still had adequate training data.[59][60] In software development, Peng et al. found a 56% increase in speed when programmers used GitHub Copilot for JavaScript tasks, while Cui et al. reported a 26% increase in task completion rate for broader software development with the same tool.[62][63] Jabarian and Henkel found that a generative AI voice agent used in job interviews produced a 17% increase in job starts and an 18% increase in retention rate.[62] Field experiments in online retail involving millions of users found that GenAI-based enhancements significantly increased sales, with treatment effects ranging from 0% to 16.3% depending on GenAI's marginal contribution relative to existing firm practices.[64] Among workers who used generative AI in the previous week, 20.5% reported saving four hours or more, 20.1% reported three hours, 26.4% reported two hours, and 33.0% reported one hour or less.[65] The average time savings amounted to 5.4% of work hours, equivalent to roughly 2.2 hours per week for someone working 40 hours.[65] (also mirrored) When all workers, including nonusers, were factored in, generative AI saved 1.4% of total work hours.[65] Feeding these self-reported time savings into a standard aggregate production model, researchers estimated that generative AI use represented a potential 1.1% increase in U.S. productivity by the second half of 2024 relative to pre-ChatGPT levels, implying that workers were on average 33% more productive in each hour they used generative AI.[65] (also mirrored) Pooled data from February, May, and August 2025 surveys indicated that generative AI time savings were equivalent to 1.6% of all work hours across the entire workforce, suggesting that generative AI may have increased labor productivity by up to 1.3% since ChatGPT was introduced.[67] Private-sector estimates of aggregate productivity growth from AI adoption range from 0.1 to 0.6 percentage points annually between 2022 and 2040, while Goldman Sachs projects 1.5 percentage points annually for the ten years following widespread adoption.[68] (also mirrored) Freelance labor markets show early signs of disruption, with one study finding that freelancers in occupations more exposed to generative AI experienced a 2% decline in the number of contracts and a 5% drop in earnings following the release of new AI software in 2022.[70] In a preregistered online experiment, Noy et al. assigned occupation-specific, incentivized writing tasks to 453 college-educated professionals and randomly exposed half to ChatGPT, finding that the average time taken decreased by 40% and output quality rose by 18%.[58] The effects were sharply heterogeneous: novice and low-skilled workers improved by 34%, while experienced and high-skilled workers saw minimal or even negative effects.[61] AI assistance improves the experience of work along several dimensions, with customers more polite and less likely to ask to speak to a manager.[59] Survey-based evidence indicates that 28% of all U.S. workers used generative AI at work to some degree as of August 2024, with 9% reporting daily use and 14% using it at least one but not every workday.[65] Survey results showed overall generative AI adoption at 54.6%, with work adoption rising from 33.3% to 37.4% over the last twelve months.[67]
24	
25	## Skill Polarization, Wages, and Inequality
26	
27	Using the AIOI measure, they provided evidence that occupations impacted by AI experience a small but positive change in wages, but no change in employment.[71] The positive wage correlation is driven primarily by occupations with higher software skill requirements, suggesting that access to complementary skills and technologies plays an important role in determining AI's labor market impact.[71] Higher-income occupations exhibit a strong positive relationship between AI impact and both employment and wages, indicating that AI has the potential to exacerbate labor market polarization.[71] This occupational, industry, and geographic exposure dataset was published in the Strategic Management Journal, volume 42, issue 12, in December 2021.[22] Tracking AI adoption from 2010 to 2023, research co-authored by an MIT Sloan associate professor found that AI's impact is often on specific tasks within jobs rather than on whole occupations.[3] When AI can perform most of the tasks that make up a particular job, the share of people in that role within a company falls by about 14%.[3] Even workers in high-wage roles heavily exposed to AI—positions near the top of the pay scale—saw their share of total employment grow by about 3% over five years, because AI boosted firm productivity.[3] Top-paying roles—including management analysts, aerospace engineers, and computer and information research scientists—saw employment within firms fall by about 3.5% over five years.[3] Business, financial, architecture, and engineering jobs shrank by about 2% to 2.5% over five years because they have a high share of tasks that match what AI can do.[3] Legal jobs gained the most from AI, with a predicted 6.4% increase in employment, as they face little direct impact from automation and are often in firms that use AI heavily.[3] This pattern contrasts with earlier technology waves such as computerization and factory automation in the 1980s through early 2000s, which mainly displaced middle-skill, routine jobs like clerical work and basic bookkeeping.[3] Korinek and Stiglitz examined artificial intelligence and its implications for income distribution and unemployment, contributing to the theoretical understanding of how AI may reshape economic inequality.[72] Financial resources, mindset, and a mismatch between graduate skills and job market demands further complicate the integration of advanced technologies.[73] In contrast, affordable metropolitan areas—at the bottom tercile—faced just a 2.3 percentage point decline in the share of clerical jobs.[74] Similarly, one additional technology requirement is associated with a 4.2 percentage point increase in the likelihood that the ad requires a certain minimum experience level and is not an entry-level position.[74] The growing integration of AI dialogue systems in educational settings raises concerns about cognitive effects, with a systematic review published in 2024 examining how over-reliance on these systems affects students' cognitive abilities.[75] In healthcare settings, ambient listening and transcription technologies powered by AI are being integrated into electronic health record documentation, raising questions about informed consent and the patient-clinician relationship.[76] The study received 239 Scopus citations.[22] Firms are actively integrating AI, particularly for automation and Industry 4.0, with the automation of repetitive and simple tasks being widespread.[73] The review searched the Scopus database on 14 November 2023, with a time restriction set from 2020.[73] Research showed that one additional technology requirement listed in a job ad is associated with an increase in the likelihood that a job will require a college degree by 3.9 percentage points.[74]

29	## Policy Implications and Institutional Adaptations
30	
31	The same report projected that over two-thirds of skills considered important in current job requirements would change within five years, with a third of essential skills in 2025 consisting of technology competencies not yet regarded as crucial to today's job requirements.[77] Yet the European Commission estimated that less than two in five adults participate in learning every year in the EU, a rate insufficient to support the needs of Industry 4.0 and beyond.[77] The OECD found that the share of the employed population with AI skills is small—at most 0.3% of those employed in OECD countries on average—but growing rapidly.[78] Research cited by the OECD found that a one standard deviation increase in exposure to AI is associated with a 0.4 percentage point increase in wage growth, largely driven by occupations requiring high familiarity with software.[78] However, the OECD case studies found that so far AI has led to little wage changes, and men—particularly in finance—were more likely than women to expect a wage increase and less likely to expect a decrease, suggesting AI may put further pressure on existing wage inequalities.[78] The same report examined how firms implementing AI say they provide training to employees but concluded that more training may be necessary, and that AI has the potential to improve adult learning systems though risks exist.[79] The OECD devoted a chapter to ensuring trustworthy AI in the workplace, covering both soft law approaches and legislation for regulating AI in employment settings.[79] A further chapter addressed social dialogue and collective bargaining in the age of AI, examining what is different for social dialogue during the AI transition and the role of social partners in shaping it.[79] Policy tools modeled included unemployment insurance and active labor market policies, recognizing that employed workers are not fully insured against idiosyncratic income risk and attempt to self-insure through precautionary saving.[80] Scholars of AI governance have argued that automation could exacerbate income and social inequalities as high- and low-skilled occupations benefit from increased wage premiums while middle-skilled jobs are phased out.[81] The high degree of uncertainty and complexity in the AI landscape imposes challenges for governments in designing effective policies, requiring both specific governance mechanisms for sectors such as healthcare and transport and a broader global governance framework for AI.[81] Research on AI governance has highlighted that whether AI develops in socially beneficial or problematic ways largely depends on public policies, governance arrangements, and how the framing of risks and uncertainties shapes regulatory responses across countries.[81] A randomized control trial of hybrid working involving 1,612 employees in a Chinese technology company found that a hybrid schedule reduced quit rates by one-third without affecting performance grades or promotions over two years.[82] A review of AI's effects on student well-being in higher education identified concerns including digital fatigue, loneliness, technostress, and reduced face-to-face interactions, advocating for balanced AI integration that supports both academic success and holistic well-being.[83] The effects are highly gendered, with more than double the share of women potentially affected by automation.[84] The OECD Employment Outlook dedicated a chapter to skill needs and policies, noting that changes in skill needs call for new training opportunities and that existing public policies supporting training for AI are not sufficient.[79] Cazzaniga and others (2024) assume that AI shocks would reduce the labor share by 5.5 percentage points based on the historical change observed in the United Kingdom between 1980 and 2014.[80] The reduction in quit rates was significant for non-managers, female employees, and those with long commutes.[82] The evidence highlights several policy-relevant dimensions, including varying AI effects across country income groups and demographic segments, the impact of algorithmic management on job quality and related policy suggestions, labor market frictions such as skill mismatch and policy tools like unemployment insurance, and the role of policy in promoting AI use that complements human labour and broadly shared productivity gains.[84][78][80] (also mirrored)

33	## Cross-Study Synthesis and Evidence Contradictions
34	
35	ings in the last two years.[85] One analysis characterizes the current state of research on AI and the labor market as "inconclusive," arguing that claims about harmful impacts on particular groups of workers are premature.[86] Similarly, the share of businesses reporting that AI replaced a "Large Number" of tasks stood at merely 3.1%, up from 2.4% six months prior.[87] Early labor market data following ChatGPT's November 2022 launch reveals a bifurcation pattern: job postings for automation-vulnerable roles decreased 13% while demand for augmentation-prone positions increased 20% through March 2025.[88] This bifurcation finding suggests a restructuring of labor demand rather than wholesale displacement, favoring roles where human judgment complements algorithmic capability.[88] However, the causal interpretation of these posting trends is contested; one analysis notes that the timing of the decline in job postings corresponds better to the macroeconomic shift of rising interest rates than to the launch of large language models.[86] Independent research by Frank et al. (2026) found similar results, reinforcing the possibility that observed labor market changes may reflect broader economic conditions rather than AI-specific effects.[86] The Congressional Budget Office emphasizes that the direction, size, and timing of AI's effects on employment, wages, and income distribution remain uncertain, with some budgetary effects occurring relatively quickly and others taking longer.[89] This uncertainty is compounded by the observation that existing research focuses on occupations that exist today, while AI might bring forth entirely new occupations or create new tasks not yet measurable.[86] A systematic literature review synthesizes the multifaceted nature of AI's employment impact, noting new opportunities from high-skilled job creation and rising interdisciplinary talent demand alongside challenges from low-skilled job replacement and employment structure polarization.[90] A capability-deployment verification gap persists, as four companies demonstrated higher-level experimental AI capabilities but could not integrate them into production workflows because adequate output verification mechanisms were absent.[91] Four recurring barriers shape this gap: context window limitations of large language models, under-performance on proprietary programming languages and protocols, non-determinism incompatible with qualification standards, and data confidentiality concerns.[91] The Congressional Budget Office notes that AI's ability to identify patterns and respond to queries in complex scenarios could affect economic growth through multiple channels, but whether AI will prove as economically transformational as some suggest remains to be seen.[89][87] This gap underscores the potential for significant job loss exceeding productivity gains, demanding careful policy intervention.[85] At the firm level, qualitative evidence from interviews with sixteen practitioners across twelve companies reveals that agentic AI adoption remains at early stages, with seven companies operating at Level 1 (AI Assistants), four at Level 2 (AI Compensators), and only one at Level 3 (Multi-Agent Orchestration).[91] This gap leaves human-in-the-loop as the only trusted verification mechanism.[91] One analysis observes that previous waves of automation primarily affected manufacturing and routine manual tasks, whereas generative AI has demonstrated remarkable fluency with knowledge work, drafting emails.[88] The references include research on the occupational impact of AI by Felten, Raj, and Seamans (2019) and firm-level studies by Babina et al. (2024) published in the Journal of Financial Economics, volume 151.[89]

37	## Conclusions and Research Gaps
38	
39	Three reasons underlie this assessment: early research findings on AI's effects on labor demand are collectively inconclusive, current data provide only weak signals about future impacts, and existing research covers only one corner of the vast landscape of urgent AI research questions.[86] LLM adoption among U.S. workers increased rapidly from 30.1% as of December 2024 to 38.3% as of December 2025, with adoption concentrated among younger, more highly educated, and higher income individuals in industries such as customer service, marketing, and information technology.[92] (also mirrored) Despite this rapid adoption, one study estimated only small effects on wages in more exposed occupations since the November 2022 public release of ChatGPT, and found no significant effects on job openings or total jobs.[92] (also mirrored) The Congressional Budget Office noted that AI could affect economic growth, employment, wages, and the distribution of income, but that the direction, size, and timing of those effects are uncertain, with some budgetary effects occurring relatively quickly and others taking longer.[89] An IMF analysis found that advanced economies will experience the benefits and pitfalls of AI sooner, and that in advanced economies the share of employment in high-exposure, high-complementarity occupations ranges between 20.2 and 37.3 percent, while the share in high-exposure, low-complementarity occupations ranges between 25.9 and 46.1 percent.[94] In emerging market economies, the ranges shift to 5.7–28.2 percent for high-exposure, high-complementarity occupations and 10.4–34.7 percent for high-exposure, low-complementarity occupations, with low-exposure occupations representing 46.1–75.9 percent of employment.[94] A joint study by the International Labour Organization and Poland's National Research Institute found that 3.5% of men's jobs in high-income countries fall into the highest automation risk category, and that in 2023 the share of jobs with high automation potential was 7.8% for women compared to 2.9% for men.[95] In a pessimistic scenario, one analysis estimated that the growth effects of generative AI will be only 0.07% per year over the coming decade, highlighting the wide range of forecasts that remain unresolved.[96] A systematic literature review concluded that the impact of AI on employment is multifaceted, with new opportunities arising from the emergence of high-skilled jobs and rising demand for interdisciplinary talent, alongside challenges from the replacement of low-skilled jobs and polarization of the employment structure.[90] Earlier technology waves such as computerization and factory automation in the 1980s through early 2000s mainly displaced middle-skill routine jobs, contrasting with AI's greater exposure of high-paying roles.[3] AI's impact on the labor market appears to operate at the task level rather than eliminating whole occupations, though the evidence on its overall effects remains inconclusive.[3][86] One analysis notes that the evidence on how AI is affecting the labor market today is inconclusive and that claims about harmful impacts on particular groups of workers are premature.[86] In low-income countries, low-exposure occupations account for 54–96.1 percent of employment.[94] By 2025, both figures had risen to 9.6% for women and 3.5% for men.[95] The demand for skills driven by AI is moving towards higher levels of expertise and multidisciplinary integration.[90] Existing research on AI's impact on the labor market has barely scratched the surface, leaving important questions about AI's effects on the labor market still unanswered.[86]

42	
43	The Brynjolfsson, Li, and Raymond customer-support agent study is detailed under Productivity Gains from Generative AI at Work.[97][98] In their preferred specification with agent and tenure fixed effects, the deployment of AI increased resolutions per hour by 0.30 chats, representing a 13.8% gain, while average handle time decreased by 3.8 minutes, a 9% decline from the baseline mean.[97][98] Notably, less experienced and lower-skilled workers improved both the speed and quality of their output, while the most experienced and highest-skilled workers saw small gains in speed and small declines in quality, a pattern that contrasts with skill-biased technical change observed in earlier waves of computer technology.[97][98] (also mirrored) However, the report noted that only 9.3% of companies had used generative AI in production during the previous two weeks as of a recent US survey, and if current AI use cases were expanded across the economy, only approximately 2.5% of US employment would be at risk of displacement.

(2025) illustrate two contrasting trajectories: in a rapid-adoption scenario, global total factor productivity grows by 2.4% in ten years, leading to a 4% increase in global GDP, while in a slower-adoption scenario, GDP growth is limited to around 1.3% with TFP growing by only 1.8%.[103] (also mirrored) A one-standard-deviation increase in firm-level AI investment was associated with a 3.7% increase in the share of college-educated workers, a 2.9% increase in master's-degree holders, and a corresponding 7.2% decline in the share of workers without a college degree.[105] (also mirrored)

46	## Limitations
47	
48	Limitations: The corpus is skewed toward lower-tier material, with only 6% of sources classified as T1 primary studies while T4 and T6 each account for 21%, leaving higher-tier evidence thin. The pipeline did not detect any cross-source contradictions, and no not-comparable pairings were flagged for screening. The telemetry provides no date range, so the temporal evidence horizon cannot be bounded from these data. Additionally, 15% of sources remain in the UNKNOWN tier, representing an unresolved coverage gap that limits confidence in the overall distribution.

    """P1-A7 (I-arch-007): the cross-section anti-restatement CONSOLIDATION pass.

    Read at CALL TIME. Default ON (unset => ON), coherent with the A20
    WEIGHT-AND-CONSOLIDATE redesign default — so a fact restated across sections is
    CONSOLIDATED into ONE primary statement + cross-references that KEEP every source's
    citation (the ``fact_dedup`` cross-reference rewrite), rather than left as duplicated
    prose. §-1.3 (CONSOLIDATE, don't DROP): repetition is corroboration; the pass keeps
    all sources as a cross-referenced multi-citation, it does not delete a corroborating
    source. An explicit ``PG_ANTI_RESTATEMENT=0`` (off/false/no) SKIPS the consolidation
    pass entirely => restated prose passes through untouched (all sources kept verbatim,
    no cross-ref rewrite) — the byte-identical no-consolidation escape hatch.

    SCOPE NOTE (honest residual): the consolidate-vs-drop semantics inside the pass live
    in ``generator/fact_dedup.py`` (``rewrite_redundant_sentences`` / ``apply_rewrites``),
    which is OUTSIDE this campaign's editable set. This predicate wires the named flag at

    the pass already KEEPS restated facts as cross-references (the drop-to-PRIMARY-only
    branch is a safe-fail fallback that fires ONLY on an LLM rewrite failure)."""
    return (

def _apply_depth_targets(template: str) -> str:
    """Rank7: make the section writer's LENGTH and CITATION-DIVERSITY targets configurable.

    There is no word governor on this code path — ``PG_TARGET_TOTAL_WORDS`` and friends are consumed
    only by ``agents/synthesizer.py`` / ``synthesis/section_writer.py``, which this module does not
    import. Report length is emergent: n_sections x rule-#8's sentence target x ~30 words/sentence.
    Rule #8's "10-18" was written as a FLOOR-raiser against the model's natural 6-8, but it functions
    as the binding CEILING: evidence-rich sections already render 16-18 sentences, i.e. the top of the
    band. This makes that band the single tunable it always was.

    Rule #9 moves WITH rule #8 on purpose. The B11 same-span dedup pass
    (``verified_compose.dedup_same_span_sentences``, default ON) keeps only one sentence per distinct
    ``(ev_id, start, end)`` footprint, so extra sentences that re-word an ALREADY-CITED span are
    deleted. Raising the sentence target without raising the distinct-source floor would invite the
    model to pad from the same spans and B11 would silently eat the gain — the exact no-op shape that

    benchmark is byte-identical. Set => FAILS LOUD if an anchor drifted, so a future template edit

            )
    if sources:
        out, n = re.subn(_RULE9_ANCHOR, f"at least {sources} DISTINCT sources", out)
        if n != 1:
            raise RuntimeError(
                f"{_DISTINCT_SOURCES_ENV} set but the rule-#9 anchor {_RULE9_ANCHOR!r} fired {n} "
                "times (expected exactly 1). The section template changed; update _apply_depth_targets."

content.text:
VERDICT: **PARTLY** — the direction is real, but two of your three headline numbers are inflated, your claimed mechanism (rule #9) demonstrably did not operate, and roughly a third of the added words are not new information. This is not an artifact and not a padded regression; it is a genuine depth gain wearing exaggerated numbers.

## 1. The headline numbers, corrected

**"107 vs 63 sources" is bibliography-entry inflation, not source diversity.** The 107 bib entries collapse to 92 unique URLs, 82 markers actually cited in prose, and ~73 distinct works by title-matching — itself still inflated by mirror-splitting. Brynjolfsson/Li/Raymond alone occupies **six** bib slots in RANK7 ([59],[60],[61],[97],[98],[99] — report.md:109-111,147-149); "GPTs are GPTs" occupies five ([26]-[29],[102]); the Hui et al. short-term-effects paper four ([42]-[45]). CTRL: 63 entries → 48 works, 40 cited. Real ratio is ~73 vs ~40 distinct works — the direction holds, the number doesn't.

**The killer counter-metric: distinct-works-per-1000-words FELL, ~11.8 (CTRL) → ~9.5 (RANK7).** Insight density per word declined. RACE grades density, not totals.

**"Rule #9 drove diversity" is refuted by the report itself.** Distinct refs per section in RANK7's final text: 7, 8, 17, 5, 13, 8, 8, 7, 8, 8. The 15-distinct-source floor is met in **1 of 10 sections**. "Empirical Evidence on Employment" has 17 verified sentences on **5** distinct refs. "Theoretical Foundations" has 28 sentences on 8 refs, **10 of them citing [1]** — one Acemoglu-Restrepo paper restated ten ways ("displacement effect… productivity effect… reinstatement effect… present such a framework… builds on prior work… The net impact of automation on labor demand thus depends on how the displacement and productivity effects weigh against each other.[1]" — report.md:11). Rule #9 at 15 is unsatisfiable against current per-section evidence slices, and the observed behavior when a rule is unsatisfiable is exactly what you feared: the model silently ignores it and pads from the sources it has, in span-distinct rewordings B11 cannot see.

## 2. Padding fraction: ~30-35% of the ADDED words; ~20-25% of the whole report

Of 187 verified sentences, I count ~40-45 that carry no new information: cross-section duplicates (~20), metadata-as-content (~8), off-topic strays (~7), broken fragments (2), plus content-free wrap-ups. Worst offenders, verbatim:

**Cross-section near-duplicates (the B11-invisible mode, confirmed present):**
- [90] stated twice, verbatim-grade: "A systematic literature review synthesizes the multifaceted nature of AI's employment impact, noting new opportunities from high-skilled job creation… alongside challenges from low-skilled job replacement and employment structure polarization.[90]" (report.md:35) vs "A systematic literature review concluded that the impact of AI on employment is multifaceted, with new opportunities arising from the emergence of high-skilled jobs… alongside challenges from the replacement of low-skilled jobs and polarization of the employment structure.[90]" (report.md:39).
- Hamilton [86] "inconclusive… claims about harmful impacts on particular groups of workers are premature" — report.md:35 AND report.md:39, near-verbatim, plus a third paraphrase in the same Conclusions paragraph.
- CBO "direction, size, and timing… remain uncertain, with some budgetary effects occurring relatively quickly and others taking longer"[89] — report.md:35 AND :39.
- MIT Sloan 14% task-level claim — report.md:7, again :27, thesis restated a third time :39.
- **Different-source duplicates, invisible to every dedup you have:** Eloundou 46% via [26] (report.md:15) and again via [102] (:44); ILO 9.6%-female/3.5%-male via [33] (:15) and via [95] (:39); Felten software-skill wage result via [71] (:27) and via OECD [78] (:31).

**Verified-but-vacuous (strict_verify checks grounding, not informativeness):** "This occupational, industry, and geographic exposure dataset was published in the Strategic Management Journal, volume 42, issue 12, in December 2021.[22]"; "The study received 239 Scopus citations.[22]"; "The review searched the Scopus database on 14 November 2023…[73]"; "The references include research on the occupational impact of AI by Felten, Raj, and Seamans (2019)…[89]" (citing another paper's reference list); Korinek cited to a **Google Scholar profile page** ([72], report.md:122).

**Off-topic on the "clean" pool:** the hybrid-WFH quit-rate RCT [82], ambient-listening EHR ethics [76], AI dialogue systems and student cognition [75], student well-being/digital fatigue [83] — none is labor-market restructuring.

**Artifacts:** 27 "(also mirrored)" markers in prose (CTRL: 3); two mid-word truncations opening sections — "ings in the last two years.[85]" (report.md:35) and "stionnaire developed by the researchers.[100][101]" (:44); and the fact_dedup pass leaked its own cross-reference into the final report: "The Brynjolfsson, Li, and Raymond customer-support agent study is detailed under Productivity Gains from Generative AI at Work.[97][98]" (:43).

That said: the majority of added material IS new — MIT Sloan firm-level results, Autor ATM/O-ring, the meta-analytic null, Chen & Wang augmenting-vs-displacing patents, Copilot 56%/26%, KDI Korea, ILO digital-divide 441.8M/66.9M, IMF SDN exposure ranges, Babina education-share shifts, the Australia tipping-point model, and a genuinely good contested-causality passage (posting declines vs interest-rate timing, report.md:35). In information terms the 2.27x is more like ~1.9x.

## 3. The dedup defense is false comfort — proven by your own log

`/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/logs/rank7_depth_compose.log:4310`: `fact_dedup: groups=16 redundants=20 rewrites_proposed=20 rewrites_kept=1 rewrites_dropped_by_strict_verify=19`. The pipeline **detected** 16 duplicate-fact groups, attempted 20 consolidations, and strict_verify killed 19 of the 19 rewrites — the consolidation pass effectively no-oped (1/20). B11 fired once all run (log:4176, 2 sentences). Everything I quoted above sailed through both passes.

## 4. The 178 drops: mostly healthy over-generation, but the tail is exhaustion

Per-section kept_fraction (log:4318-4327): theory 0.67, prod 0.61, skill 0.60, occ 0.58, policy 0.56, empl 0.52 — healthy trim. Then: synthesis 0.44, **conclusions 0.36, additional 0.36**. The thematic sections over-generate benignly; the tail sections scrape an exhausted pool and the verifier amputates half. Also: "fired only ONCE" undersells it — the floor retried only the intro (0.32, log:4046), yet **two sections ended below 0.40 with no retry logged**. Either they're exempt or the floor measures at a different point; either way two-fifths-kept tail sections shipped. The amputations also explain the coherence damage: sections opening "An alternative formulation of the same study reports…" (:23, no antecedent), "Conversely, among occupations with low computer use…" (:19), "Using the AIOI measure, they provided evidence…" (:27, "they" never introduced), and "In contrast, affordable metropolitan areas—at the bottom tercile—faced just a 2.3 percentage point decline" (:27) whose other half was verifier-killed.

## 5. RACE scores (1-5)

| Dimension | CTRL | RANK7 |
|---|---|---|
| Comprehensiveness | 2.5 | 4 |
| Insight/depth | 2.5 | 3 |
| Instruction-following | 3 | 3 |
| Readability | 2.5 | 2 |

Instruction-following note: the task says "Focus on how AI, as a key driver of the **Fourth Industrial Revolution**…" — the phrase appears **zero times** in either report's body (RANK7's one grep hit is a bibliography title). Your own outline checklist flagged it as a gap (log:1342) and it shipped unaddressed.

## 6. Next ceiling and the one lever

Raising 28-36 higher is exhausted: tail kept_fraction already 0.36, fact_dedup already detecting duplicate-fact groups, rule #9 already ignored 9/10 times. The binding constraint is **distinct usable evidence per section, not sentence count**. The one lever: **more sections (13-14) with disjoint per-section evidence allocation** — give each section its own basket slice so 28-36 sentences draw on ~15 genuinely distinct works instead of milking 8; this attacks exactly the tail-exhaustion and single-source-milking failure modes, and 13 sections x ~750 words clears 10k. The one falsifying number: **distinct-works-cited per 1000 words**. RANK7 is ~9.5, CTRL ~11.8. If the next run lands above ~11 at 10k+ words, the lever worked; if it stays ≤9.5, you bought words with restatement again and the lever is wrong.

Bottom line: this is your first run that is NOT an artifact dressed as a finding — the prompt line was a real ceiling and the report is genuinely deeper. But report it honestly: ~1.9x information gain (not 2.27x), ~73 distinct works (not 107), source density per word DOWN, rule #9 unmet in 9/10 sections, consolidation pass 1-for-20, and a readability regression that RACE will charge you for.
